speaker: interviewee
text: --saved my life, this girl. Oy, she was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: June 17, 1996. Survivor, Gabriel Krause. Mr. Krause, you were telling me that you were on your way to the south.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wound up in [INAUDIBLE]. I won't go to Israel. This way maybe I can go through the border some way-- some way. In the meantime, I've lined up a job in this place. And it was a-- a-- a place for kids, what they left from the war. Orphans-- orphanage. In fact--

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was an orphanage. What they got one-- one-- no parents, or no parents at all. Or they got one parent. So a-- one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went over there. And she tell me that she was a-- a-- a lady, she run this business over there. She tell me, you can do everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can do her repairs. And I do some repairs, everything-- everything for a piece of bread with soup, and something like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. Not the janitor. I was all around, working. And I got care-- take care of this, what's over there. Especially this-- for the-- for the-- for the nursery, repair chairs. Everything, I do everything. And-- and this was important for the people. Well, they don't have men, no men over there, everything in the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: So over there was a lot of kids. And one of the kids was a very small kids, comparing to other kids. Well, everybody was so high, he was so small. So a blond, nice-looking kid. And all of a sudden, show up Sunday, show up his mother, this kid's mother. And I know this is the kid's mother. She was a nice-looking woman. And she tell me, I ask her, this is your son? And she tell me yeah. And I'm a widow, because my-- my-- my-- my husband was killed, or died in the war. And she was one year in summer-- in the-- in the Siege of Leningrad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she survive because they take her out. After a year she were-- she was 70 pounds. And these kids was a skeleton. And because they advocated her because of her specialty, together with this-- because she work in the-- in the special place with 250 people, she was the chief of 250 people. And this-- they take it out from the-- the-- the Lake Ladoga was frozen. They take out, little by little, the people. The year she was in these places, you know, everybody starving. And when they stay longer, she'll be starving. And anyway, they take it out and they bring her to summer camp, in this place. And she working with the machinery over there, in this place again. And the kids was in this-- because she-- the kids that were specially, she was in this--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I met the mother, She's a nice-looking woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was a very nice-looking woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lola. And I tell them, this is your son. Yeah, then I got over there a girl, what she serve the kids the food. So I tell the girl, please, for this small guy, give him a little more soup or a [NON-ENGLISH]. Give him a little [NON-ENGLISH] more, because I know his mother. And so anyway, for this kid-- kid-- for this I must do some complimentary work for this girl. So anyway, do something, work this way and the other way. So the kids got a little more [NON-ENGLISH]. And these kids-- I mean, there was-- and the mother was satisfied. These kids were-- was not so hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I was more with the mother. And we'd go out. And these kids tell me-- this may-- this will be my father. I find a father. This was my father. So and they were some nice-looking kids, very intelligent kids. And they say, it was my father. Now she tell me I got a father. And my-- and my wife was-- she was very happy with this. So you married the woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married this woman, at the end. And then I cannot marry. She got a conference with people was working this-- in this-- because came a law. Came out a law in 1945, the end of '45, came out a special law. And special treaty with the Polish government. In ex-- not in exile, the Polish communist government in Poland, they take out the Polish citizen from-- from these places, and they would go back to Poland. And every citizen that I was-- and my papers was I was not a Jew, originally Polack, from Warsaw. And she must make a decision. She won't go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she ask the people what working, some was the Jews, in the highest positions. And they tell her Lola, you got a chance to go out from here, from this Russia, from Russia, marry him. Better you go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, she was a nice-looking lady. I talk to her. You-- you-- your decision. Stay here or you leave-- you leave here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she make the decision. She talk over these people. And the people tell her, nobody knows. Because they-- they-- [INAUDIBLE]. And she accept it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to the office. They call it [INAUDIBLE], pay five rubles. She signed the papers. She's my wife, official. And then I put on her, with the boy, on my papers. And then I can go out from Russia. And she can go from Russia. And when it came the time in 1945, '46. That's the beginning of '46, I think. No, it's the end of '45 or '46. I got the papers. And I go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: With them. And I prepare everything. And we travel 10,000 miles from this place, to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: The child's was-- was-- he changed the name now. It's funny, because he changed the name. I don't know what the name was before.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first name was-- wait a minute there. Because he changed something. No, she didn't change from-- it was [? Markowitz, ?] [? Markowitz. ?] Left Mark. They left it Mark.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they put on Mark, a bit better. Because he gets-- the Russian name is Mark [? Markowitz, ?] something. So I tell him make it short, Mark, Mark Krause, if I get it. And that's so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mark, Lola and-- and you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did-- where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Poland. We went to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Poland, to this territories, what was given, they take away from Germany to-- to Poland. And they call this-- the place called is [INAUDIBLE]. Is this was a place where it's a-- this is in Silesia. And when we came to Poland on the borders, Poland was fighting each other. There was the enemy, Krajowa Army. It was fighting the Communist Army, Polish Communist Army. They're fighting. They kill the Jews, meantime. So our train was always shot-- shooting this train. Because people coming for Russia, communists.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they're shooting and also-- so they take the trains with these people to this place, what was before Germany. And they take the Polacks-- got the territory. And they take these people over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the-- the-- the situation was very fluid over there. Because the Jewish people was in bad shape-- bad situation. You never know what can happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened in-- it happened in Kielce, in a Polish city. They accused the-- the Jews of taking blood from the-- from the-- from the Polack.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were where, at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was not-- I just hear about this. I was not far from Kielce. And they kill all of these-- for the people, there was a kibbutz over there, for the people, boys and girls, they kill them off with hacks, with the-- kick them, and that's-- they kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They told you that when-- when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they told me they kill all the-- in this kibbutz was liquidated by Polacks. There was a pogrom and they kill them over there. This-- this-- they came from-- they came-- the survivors came from-- from Russia, from some places, from Siberia, from the other places. I was over there too, in some Siberia, Tymen'-Aryk in Siberia. So they came from this kind of place, the survivors. They form a kibbutz in Kielce. They live over there. They prepare to go to Israel someday. They came. They accuse them of something. They-- they kill a-- a boy and they take blood from others, something like they kill them, 40 people there, died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my-- we stay in the [INAUDIBLE] over there. We stay. I was-- we organize this kibbutzim. And we got-- I always stay with a gun. Because it was a dangerous, dangerous situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got contacts with the Bricha, from Israel. They came the-- the soldiers from Israel, they came. And they will-- will start to organize all this, immigration, little by little to Israel. And not legal. Unlegal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not legal, yeah. So I belong to some organization with this unlegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this, the organization, we call it [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this organization do something, help-- help to do something. Because Israel fight for life. It was war with these Arabs. And they need ammunition. They need help. And the English stay on the-- they don't give us help. They try to disturb this, everything. No immigration, no nothing. And beside, they got all spies from the CIA. I forgot the name of this organization, of the English organization. And they spy on us. And when they-- they spy and they-- they don't-- they disrupt immigration. They disrupt everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your function? What did you have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their function is to kill somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- you get-- somebody gave you orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who give them the orders. No, I-- they came and then they-- they give me the gun. And I supposed to liquidate someone. The end, I don't liquidate it because she got pregnant. My wife got pregnant. And I was a little-- not in bad-- so when they come, I supposed to do some--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in bad shape. Very bad-- she got bad pregnancy.

speaker: interviewee
text: She vomit and she got all kind of problems.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sick, like I don't know what it were. I went with the professor, some big professor. He tell me, she got two-- two kids, a German professor. I give him cigarettes, for this, he tell me. She's too-- she got-- fact, she got water. She got a lot of water. And her stomach was like a balloon. And the situation that I got-- they want us to do something as opposed to liquidate somebody. So this--

speaker: interviewer
text: So who-- the organization that gave you the orders was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I-- they call me. They're supposed to give me orders, where I supposed to go do the job. So it was a room-- small room like this, dark and three people sitting on the table. And they put the lights on my eyes. And they tell me why you're supposed to do this and this and this. So I explain my wife is in bad shape. She's sickly, very sick. And I got on my-- my-- this-- I-- when I suppose to do this job, maybe my hand be shaking. It'll be no good. Because when they do job-- do a clear job, will knock him out for nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it will be no good. So they discuss between, this thing. I don't see the people. Because it's all dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: They just-- with the electric lamp, to me, in my eyes. So they discuss, they tell me-- I got a number, just give me the parabellum back. I put it on the table. Turn around, and I went out. So I don't do the job.

words.text: So I don't do the job.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Wasseralfingen. Yeah, that's a German town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And before we came to Germany, this take a long time too. [LAUGHS] Before we came to Germany. Because from Poland to Germany was a long, long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I belonged to the Betar, in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, [INAUDIBLE], and I belonged to a [INAUDIBLE] organization. And my-- and my superior was Menachem Begin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know at the time that he was your superior?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know, but Menachem Begin, I know him. He was an Israel. He-- the war-- he was in hiding too. So all this came from somewhere, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: After-- after this episode, where did you go? Did you go to a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we was in the DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was in DP camp. Yeah, we was in DP camps, yeah. We was in DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any special job at the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I got a special job. I work for the American Army. And-- and I work for the DP camp. We-- we cut in the by-- in the mountains by-- and we cut wood. Part of the wood go to army. Part of the wood came to our line-- to our camp, when we need wood for the-- to-- to warm up the-- the ovens, and something like this. And I was the leader. I got 14 people under me, see. And the-- the jeeps-- not jeeps. I mean the-- the-- the big trucks, it was American trucks on six walls-- six wheels. So this guy, [? Hershel, ?] was my right hand. He help me out. You know, in the mountains, I was the leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: This guy [? Hershel? ?] Who's this guy, [? Hershel? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a young boy. I put in the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that you have the picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. So I work-- was working over there. I was the leader over there. And it's a very, very responsible job. Because in the mountains, the big-- big trees, high. He always was-- he feel the-- he feel the-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --wind. So and I feel the wind, where I supposed to cut, in what place. Because it can't-- the-- the tree can't fall on you, on the peoples working. So it was-- I wet my finger and I see where the wind. That I check my finger. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I see what they're doing. Then we're doing the job. And this-- this was my job. It's a dangerous job. You know, we're doing with the army, some [INAUDIBLE]. So you used to cut the wood for the American Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we cut the woods. We got machines and the people working this, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife was pregnant. Did she have her baby at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she got the baby. Through very hard-- hardship, she got the baby in the hospital. They cut it out, from her, you see. We got a baby, but she was 12 and 1/2 pounds.

speaker: interviewee
text: The doctor that was there, they found the baby. They don't know what it is. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what-- how-- how did you name them? Name him?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you come to the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to the states in '46-- in-- no. And they came in the States, 1950.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we came in-- in 19-- --'49, December, the end of December. It was '50, almost. And this was in 1950. It's the end of this-- and we came here in this. And it was-- it was really trouble because we came here with the ship. I remember the name of the ship, General McRae. This was a ship, an army ship. And was coming from the-- Bremerhaven, from Germany, and we got-- for 12 days or 14 days it was a storm. And we cannot go from Bremerhaven because the mines. It was full of mines. And we go a long time before we [INAUDIBLE] go out from the mines. Every time, ooh, mines, mines. And later they find we went through the eye of the storm. And everybody was sick, including me. And everybody in the-- in the-- the crew was sick. It was terrible. And you cannot walk because everything was-- the floor was with-- with the vomit. And just we walk, or fall, it's unbelievable. I was thanking God we came to the New York area.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came to New York. It was a terrible storm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Krause, a person like you, with so much-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody knock at the door, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: A person like you, with so much experience in life--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of message would you-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Donna is going to just check.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, maybe tell it's-- it's out of order, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, she can stay, maybe, in the back when she want. No? Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: She-- I think she left. She's going to come later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. She can later. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: --what kind of message would you teach to future--

speaker: interviewer
text: To future generations? What kind of-- of-- you know, what piece of advice would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I give one piece of advice. Be strong. Be strong. Be strong. And don't believe in the old [INAUDIBLE], bologna, what they tell you. The world is a very, very dangerous world. It is no mercy. No mercy no place. When you don't have power, you're down, down, down. You're nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you've got power, you've got mercy. You've got life. Without this, you can't exist. And the Jewish people, this new generation what came, when they don't have the power and the moral power and the physical power, they cannot go through life at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I see with my own eyes. Without power, you cannot live. Absolutely, no place in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Power is your life. When you've got power, you've got life. And this is the situation, my situation. When I was in business-- I was in business here too-- how do I survive? Because I got power, and I got my gun here, always ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got them here too. And they know. And I got stories to tell you, if I can-- story just too long to tell, to stories, how I survive in the-- in the-- in-- in the jungle, what this-- the-- the-- this-- this whole situation changing because the white people run away. And I live with-- with black people, and what I deal with, how I deal with this kind of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: --there is a special message for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandchildren, when they can understand what I tell to them, when they can understand, they'll be OK. But not-- but they don't understand when they look for just material things, don't-- I-- I-- I don't believe in this. I don't believe in this. You must have, inside, the will to survive. And you can survive when you've got power. When you've got the power, you survive. When not, you-- nobody will be taking it [INAUDIBLE]. Power by money, power by other things. You must have this power. Otherwise you don't survive. That's what I go through.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is myself, Gabriel Krause. My father, Wolf Krause, and my older brother, Jacob Krause.

speaker: interviewee
text: And taken in Warsaw, 1915. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My name is Gabriel Krause. I am located on left side. On the right side is my-- my dear mother, Esther [? Friedman ?] Krause. The next is my brother, the tall guy, Jacob Krause. And from the right is my father, Wolf Krause. And for the left side is Misha Krause. And-- and-- and-- and Mordehai. The small one is Mordehai. And from the right side is David Krause.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the baby is-- the baby is-- the name of the baby is-- oh my god. Severin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Severin Krause, yeah. The place is Warsaw. And date of photo must be the 1925.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- my-- my name is-- now I talk about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left side, we got my brother, the younger brother, Severin Krause. He died-- he died in-- in the ghetto. He died in ghetto on the-- ghetto-- ghetto-- there, that's a ghetto. Place was the ghetto. He died--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the picture was taken in 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next one is [? Dovid. ?] And the picture was dated-- made in Warsaw in 1938. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: First name is-- it's my brother. First name is Henrik. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the place was in Warsaw. And the date of photo is 1938, made in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he died in-- he died in epidemic of typhus. This is my brother. And-- oh, first name is-- is Mordehai, Mordehai Krause. It is-- the place is Bologna, Italy. And date of photo is 1940--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife. I-- I got something in my mouth. It'll be no good. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, that's why you have to-- to bring your water.

speaker: interviewee
text: First name. My first name again? No. This is my dear wife, Lola Krause, from whom the name is [INAUDIBLE]. She was born in Vitebsk, living in-- and the place was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in-- in Vitebsk, by her father. And date of photo, must have been 1937. Put down the-- put down the machine. The first name is Lola, it's Lola Krause with her son, Mark Krause. And the photo is taken in Leningrad, in 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yeah, this is [INAUDIBLE], on the land.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The name is my wife, Lola, and me on the right side.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Gabriel, from the right side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the last name is Krause.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got my son, adopted son, Mark Krause, on the left side. And then my-- my son-- my son, Wolf Krause.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. First name, my name is Gabriel Krause. and from the left side, my son, Mark Krause. And from the right side, my Lola, Lola Krause, my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my youngest son, Wolf Krause. And the picture is done in-- in 1982.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture is done-- 19-- my first-- my name? No, his name. It is Todd Krause, [? Todder, ?] Todd, Todd, they call him Todd. And from the right side is my granddaughter, Kimberly Krause. And the place is Philadelphia. And that's-- the date of the photo is about eight-- 19-- 1978.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first name of-- the-- the name of the baby is [? Carla ?] [? Mina-- ?] [? Carla ?] [? Mina ?] Krause. And the place is Boston. And the date of photo, 1895.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1995. Hundred years old, my god, well, they go back so far. It's unbelievable. [LAUGHTER]
